other_observers: There were a number of other birders present but the only ones I knew were Chishun Kwong and Joanna Chen.; Found by Kathy Mihm Dunning.
specific_location: 100 yards above the second footbridge, right along the Gregory Canyon trail.
description: It was a tanager, same size and shape as Western Tanager, but bright fluorescent orange-red on all body feathers, with solid black wings and tail. Bill was large and pale, maybe slightly larger than the bills I am used to seeing on Western Tanager.Behaviors: Bird was foraging and singing from the tops of trees--it seemed to prefer the cottonwoods in the draw beside the trail, but also sang regularly from the dead top of a live ponderosa pine near the trail. It seemed territorial but did not appear to have a mate. There were female Western Tanagers in the area, including one in the same tree as the SCTA at nearly the same time; I never saw them interact and I never saw any indication that they were paired. The fact that the male was still singing frequently, almost continuously, at 10:00 AM on July 7th strongly suggests to me that he was unpaired.Call: Bird was singing frequently, through at least 10:00 AM when most other birds were silent. Song was almost indistinguishable from those of nearby Western Tanager males: strict series of 3-5 well-separated 2- or 3-syllabled semimusical burry phrases, delivered within each series at ca. 2/sec, the series starting a few seconds apart. I believe the song may have included a few phrases that were clearer than most Western Tanager phrases, but I was never certain I could distinguish the species by ear, even with direct audio comparison.Plumage: alternate
